Re:
Fnrn PolrrrcAl- PRACTTcEs CouurssroN
.128 J Street o Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4_232g
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
April 16,2009
Douglas L. White
Greenberg Traurig, LLP
2450 Colorado Ave, Suite 400 East
Santa Monica, CA904O4
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-079
Dear Mr. White:
This Ietter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Riverbank
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ..Act"j-i ----
QUESTTON
May members of the Riverbank City Council, who may have a conflict of interest
in some decisions related to changes in the land use element oithe proposed general plan,
participate in final decisions on approval of the general plan update and certif]cation of its
environmental impact report?
CONCLUSION
Yes. So long as the decisionmaking protocol you describe follows the dictates of
Regulation 18709 for "segmenting" decisions in which the members may have a conflict
of interest' they may participate in final decisions on approval of the general plan update
and certification of the associated environmental impaci report.
FACTS
The City of Riverbank is currently in the process of updating its General plan.
city staff has prepared, and the City's planning Commissio, hr, ,""o*mended for
approval, the certification of the proposed environmental impact report for the general
' Go'ernment Code sections 8 I 000-9 I 0 14. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109- I 8997, of the California Code of Regularions.
{ts


File No. 4-09-079
Page No. 2
plan update, as well as adoption of the general plan update itself. These two related
decisions are hereafter described collectively as "the general plan update."
The general plan update includes changes of the City's Land Use element. These
changes include, among other things, modification of some zoning designations and the
inclusion of minimum building densities. You believe that these ihanges have the
potential to create a conflict of interest for one or more members of the City Council.
The City has therefore developed a protocol for segmentation of the decisionmaking
process under Regulation 18709, similar to the protocol discussed in the Murphy Advice
Letter, No. 4-07-031. The goal is to allow any councilmember(s) who may hure u
conflict of interest in certain specific decisions to participate in discussions and votes on
final adoption of the general plan update.
You describe the city's proposed decisionmaking protocol as follows:
(l) Identification of Abstention Areas. The City would create and implement a
procedure to separately manage City Council decisions affecting specific
"abstention areas," defined as properties within 500 feet of real property in
which a given councilmember has an economic interest.
(2) Staff Presentation. When the general plan update agenda item is called, the
public hearing will be opened. Staff (including the City's consultants) will
give a presentation, at which time councilmembers may ask questions, but
will not be expected or encouraged to express any opinions.
(3) Public Testimony. After the staff presentation, public testimony will be heard,
presenting evidence, argument and opinion on the proposal. Councilmembers
may ask questions, but will not be expected or encouraged to offer opinions.
(4) Staff final comments. After the public testimony has been heard, staff will
present closing comments and answer any final questions by councilmembers.
At this point, deliberations on the proposed update will begin.
(5) Identification of Abstention Areas. "Abstention areas" foieach council-
member will first be idenrified.
(6) Matters within the Abstention Area. Assuming at least one councilmember
has identified an abstention area, the other members will be asked if any of
them would propose any change to any proposed land use designation,
including zoning designations and minimum building densities, within any of
the abstention areas (other than their own). If one or more members indicate a
wish to propose changes within an abstention area, the affected member will
step off the dais and leave the room. The proposed change will then be
discussed and the matter decided by the remaining -e*b"rr, after which the
affected member will return to the dais.
(7) Matters outside Abstention Areas. When there are no more proposed changes
to any abstention area, the councilmembers will be asked if any of them would
propose any change to any land use designation not within an abstention area.
If so, rhese changes will be discussed and decided by all members.


File No. 4-09-079
Page No. 3
(8) Changes to Goals, Policies and Implementation Measures. The members will
next be asked if they would propose any change to the text of the goals,
policies and implementation measures contained in the general plan update.
Any such proposal will be discussed and decided by all members, after which
the public hearing will be closed.
(9) After the public hearing is closed, all members will vote first on approval or
disapproval of the entire Final Environmental tmpact Report, including any
amendments necessitated by changes to the land use designations and goals,
policies and implementation measures. All members will then vote on the
entire proposed general plan update.
Under this protocol, each decision affecting an abstention area will be considered
before the decision to adopt the general plan update, with each councilmember leaving
the room before any discussion or decision affecting an abstention area in which he or
she had a potential conflict of interest. Only after all decisions affecting the abstention
areas are made will the full City Council consider adoption of the general plan update in
its final form.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict of interest provisions are designed to ensure that public
officials will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their
own financial interests or the financial interest of persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision when it is
"reasonably foreseeable" that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or
more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)
The Commission has adopted an eight-step analyical framework to determine whether a
public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a particular governmental
decision, which we apply below. (See Regulation 18700(bXl)-(8).)
Step One: Are Members of the City Council Public Officiats?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state
or local government agency." (Section 82048, Regulation 18701.) Members of the City
Council are therefore public officials within the meaning of the Act.2
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section S72OO ("872N filers" include city council
members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of eionomic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation
I 8702'5(bX I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for rhe
duration ofthe discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and


File No. A-09-079
Page No.4
Step Two: will the Members of the City Council be Making, participating in
Making, or Using their Positions to Influence a Governmental Decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation lS7OZ.l.)
A, public official "participates in making" a governmental decision when he or
she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations
regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2)3 A public official is using his or her official
position to "influence a govemmental decision" if he or she contacts or appears before, or
otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee, o, "orsuitant of the City
regarding the decisions. (Regulation 18702.3.)
The members of the City Council will be called upon to consider whether the City
should approve or disapprove amendments updating the City's general plan. Therefore
they will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using their oificial positions to
influence a governmental decision.
Step Three: Identifying the Councilmembers' Economic Interests-possible
Sources of Conflicts of Interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable the decision will have material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her-immediate
family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, (Section 87103; Regulations
18703-18703.5.) as follows:
an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section g7103(d);
Regulation I 8703. I (b));
an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation lg:i1z.S,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
I Exception: making or participating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its presciiied
governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official's wholly
owned real property or business entity. (See Regulation lgil}2.4)
a
a


File No. 4-09-079
Page No. 5
a
a
an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income
that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87 103(e); Regulation 18703.4);
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediaie
family. This is also knows as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87103, Regulation I 8703.5.)
"Indirect investment or interest" means any investment or interest owned by a
business entity in which the official owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103(e).)
Your question is confined to the sufficiency of a process designed to permit one
or more councilmembers who may have a conflict on interest in some portions of the
general plan update, due to potential effects on their economic interesti in real property,
to participate in final discussion and voting on the general plan update, after traving
recused themselves from discussion and voting on prior decisioni that might affect their
economic interests. Because the question concems only economic interesis in real
property, and you have not provided any facts concerning other possible economic
interests, our analysis is limited to the real property interests you posit.
Step Four: Are the Councilmembers'Economic Interests Directly or Indirectly
Involved in the Decisions You Describe?
R'eal property in which a public official has an economic interest is considered
directly involved in a governmental decision under any of the following circumstances:
o The property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the property
that is the subject of the decision. (Regulation tg7o4.Z(a)(l).)
o The decision involves a zoning or rezoning, annexation or de-annexation,
sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county,
district, or other local govemmental subdivision of the real property in
which the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real
property. (Regulation I 87 0a.2@)(2).)
r The decision involves the issuance, denial, or revocation of a license,
permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of the real
property in which the official has an inrerest. (Regulation Lg70a.2@)(3).)


File No. 4-09-079
Page No. 6
a
a
The decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of any taxes
or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in which the official has
an interest. (Regulation 187 0a.2@)@).)
The decision is to designate the survey area, to select the project area, to
adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to certify the
environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory
to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions, and real property in which the official has an interest or any part
of it is located within the boundaries or the proposed boundaries of the-
redevelopment area. (Regulation 187 0a.2@)(5).)
The decision involves construction of or improvements to streets, water,
sewer, stonn drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which
the official has an interest will receive new or improved services.
(Regul ation 187 0a.2@)(6).)
Real property not directly involved in a govemmental decision is indirectly
involved. (Regulation 1870a(a).) Because we do not have sufficient information from
which to conclude that the councilmembers' economic interests in real property would be
indirectly involved in decisions concerning the general plan update, we-wii begin our
analysis from an initial presumption that all of the councilmembers' real propeiy
interests would be directly involved in decisions on the general plan update. 
-
We must, however, consider this presumption of direct involvement iri light of the
decisionmaking protocol you describe. You indicate that the City Council's process will
be divided into two parts: a public hearing phase and a decisionmaking, or voting, phase.
Regulation 187 04.2(b)(3) provides as follows :
'
"(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in which a
public official has an interest is not directly involved in a govemmental
decision, but is instead indirectly involved if:
t...)
"(3) The decision solely concems the adoption or amendment of a general
plan and all of the following apply:
"(A) The decision only identifies planning objectives or is otherwise
exclusively one of policy. A decision will not quarify under this
subdivision if the decision is initiated by the public official, by a person
that is an economic interest of the publit official, or by a person
representing either the public official or an economic interest of the public
official.
a


File No. ,{-09-079
Page No. 7
"(B) The decision requires a further decision or decisions by the public
official's agency prior to implementing the planning or policy objectives.
Examples of further decisions include, but are not limited to, permitting,
licensing, rezoning, or the approval of or change to a zoning variance, land
use ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent.
"(c) The decision does not concem an identifiable parcel or parcels or
development project. A decision does not "concern an identifiable parcel
or parcels" solely because, in the proceeding before the agency in which
the decision is made, the parcel or parcels are merely included in an area
depicted on a map or diagram offered in connection with the decision,
provided that the map or diagram depicts all parcels located within the
agency's jurisdiction and economic interests of the official are not singled
out.
"(D) The decision does not concern the agency's prior, concurrent, or
subsequent approval of, or change to, a permit, license, zoning
designation, zoning variance, land use ordinance, or specific plan or its
equivalent."
Under this exception, it appears that the councilmembers' real property would be
indirectly involved in the final, voting phase of the process you describe. Thus, so long
as the final discussion and vote is restricted to broad topics above the level of specific,
individual parcels of real property, the exception articulated by this regulation provides
that real property interests that are directly involved in earlier, more specific decisions,
are only indirectly involved in the final approval of the general plan update.a
Step Five: Material Financial Effects.
Regulation 18705.2(a)(l) provides that the financial effect of a govemmental
decision on real property directly involved in a governmental decision is presumed to be
material. Pursuant to the regulation, this presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is
not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect
at all on the real property.
Under Regulation 18705.1(bXl), the financial effect of a govemmental decision
on real property indirectly involved in a govemmental decision is presumed not to be
material. This presumption can be rebutted by proof that there are specific circum-
stances regarding the decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in
which the public official has an economic interest, that make it reasonably foreseeable
that the decision willhave a material financial effect on the real property in which the
'1 If for any reason this exception cannot be applied to an economic interest otherwise directly
involved in the final part of the decision, the official would have to recuse himself or herself from
participation in the final decisionmaking, as provided in Regulation 18702.5, outlined in footnote two
above.


File No. A-09-079
Page No. 8
public official has an interest. You provide no information indicating any specific
circumstances regarding these decisions that may rebut a presumption of non-materiality.
For purposes of our analysis we will therefore assume that no such circumstances exist.
In summary, for the economic interests in real property you describe, there are
rebuttable presumptions that the economic effect of a governmental decision will be
material, or will not be material, depending on whether the economic interest is directly
or indirectly involved in the decision.
Step Six: Reasonable Foreseeability.
Whether a financial effect of a govemmental decision is reasonably foreseeable at
the time the decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. The effect of
a decision is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered "reasonably foreseeable;" a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when
providing advice. (In re Oglesby, supra,l FPPC Ops. 71.)
Your request for advice appears to raise no question on the foreseeability of the
presumed financial effects of the decisions at issue. Our analysis therefore proceeds
under the assumption that the presumptive effects of these decisions are in all cases
"reasonably foreseeable," and will not be rebutted
Your account of the facts does not suggest any potential application of two
"exceptions" to the Act's conflict of interest provisiols treated, when appropriate, in
Steps 7 and 8 of the Commission's standard analysis.s What remains to be ionsidered,
then, is whether councilmembers who may have a presumptive conflict of interest in
decisions directly affecting economic interests in real property may recuse themselves
from those decisions at an early stage of the process leading up to final decisions on
approval of the entire general plan update, and yet be permitted to participate in the final
stages of the decisionmaking process.
The decisionmaking process you describe was created with an eye to Regulation
18709, which provides for "segmentation" of certain decisions, as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the
following conditions apply:
5 Thgse "exceptions" relate only to decisions that involve the "public generally" or the "legally
required participation" of a decisionmaker who would otherwise have a conflict of interest in the decision
at issue.


File No. ,{-09-079
Page No. 9
"(l) The decision in which the official has a financiar interest can
be broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably
interrelated to the decision in which the official has a disqualifying
financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
segmented from the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's participation in any way; and
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest
has been madg, the disqualified public official's participation does not
result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from
which the official was disqualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are ,.inextricably
interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively determine,
affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision.
"(c) Budget Decisions and General Plan Adoption or Amendment
Decisions Affecting an Entire Jurisdiction: Once all the separate decisions
related to a budget or general plan affecting the entire jurisdiction have
been finalized, the public official may participate in the final vote to adopt
or reject the agency's budget or to adopt, reject, or amend the general
plan."
Under the process you have outlined, the City will "segment" decisions affecting
specific "abstention, areas" (property within 500 feet of a given official's real property
economic interest) from the overall general plan decision. Each decision affecting an
abstention area will be considered before the decision to adopt the general plan ufdates,
with each member leaving the room and not participating in any diicussion or decision
affecting the designated abstention area for which he or she has a conflict of interest.
Only after all decisions affecting the abstention areas have been resolved will the full
council convene to consider adoption of the general plan update.
The process proposed by the City meets each of the criteria outlined in Regulation
18709, and we conclude that all councilmembers, notwithstanding conflicts of interest in
decisions "segmented" as you describe, may participate in discusiion and voting on the
general plan updates at the conclusion ofthis process.
Regulation 18709(a)(3) requires that the decision in which an official has a
financial interest be considered "first." As we said in the Murphy Advice Letter, supra,
we interpret the word "first" in this regulation to mean simply that segmented decisions


File No. 4-09-079
Page No. l0
must be fully resolved before any final decision to "adopt, reject, or amend the general
plan'" More than one decision can be "segmented," so l'ong as they are decided in an
orderthat would prevent one decision from determining or'altering the result of one of
the other segmented decisions, each of which must of 
""ou.r. comply with the conditions
prescribed in Regulation 18709.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
9*z(M
By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
LTWjgl

sections.question:
May members of the Riverbank City Council, who may have a conflict of interest
in some decisions related to changes in the land use element oithe proposed general plan,
participate in final decisions on approval of the general plan update and certif]cation of its
environmental impact report?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. So long as the decisionmaking protocol you describe follows the dictates of
Regulation 18709 for "segmenting" decisions in which the members may have a conflict
of interest' they may participate in final decisions on approval of the general plan update
and certification of the associated environmental impaci report.

The City of Riverbank is currently in the process of updating its General plan.
city staff has prepared, and the City's planning Commissio, hr, ,""o*mended for
approval, the certification of the proposed environmental impact report for the general

18109- I 8997, of the California Code of Regularions.

plan update, as well as adoption of the general plan update itself. These two related
decisions are hereafter described collectively as "the general plan update."
The general plan update includes changes of the City's Land Use element. These
changes include, among other things, modification of some zoning designations and the
inclusion of minimum building densities. You believe that these ihanges have the
potential to create a conflict of interest for one or more members of the City Council.
The City has therefore developed a protocol for segmentation of the decisionmaking

conflict of interest in certain specific decisions to participate in discussions and votes on

You describe the city's proposed decisionmaking protocol as follows:
(l) Identification of Abstention Areas. The City would create and implement a
procedure to separately manage City Council decisions affecting specific

which a given councilmember has an economic interest.
(2) Staff Presentation. When the general plan update agenda item is called, the

will not be expected or encouraged to express any opinions.
(3) Public Testimony. After the staff presentation, public testimony will be heard,
presenting evidence, argument and opinion on the proposal. Councilmembers

(4) Staff final comments. After the public testimony has been heard, staff will
present closing comments and answer any final questions by councilmembers.
At this point, deliberations on the proposed update will begin.
(5) Identification of Abstention Areas. "Abstention areas" foieach council-
member will first be idenrified.
(6) Matters within the Abstention Area. Assuming at least one councilmember
has identified an abstention area, the other members will be asked if any of

including zoning designations and minimum building densities, within any of

step off the dais and leave the room. The proposed change will then be

(7) Matters outside Abstention Areas. When there are no more proposed changes
to any abstention area, the councilmembers will be asked if any of them would
propose any change to any land use designation not within an abstention area.
If so, rhese changes will be discussed and decided by all members.
(8) Changes to Goals, Policies and Implementation Measures. The members will
next be asked if they would propose any change to the text of the goals,
policies and implementation measures contained in the general plan update.
Any such proposal will be discussed and decided by all members, after which
the public hearing will be closed.
(9) After the public hearing is closed, all members will vote first on approval or
disapproval of the entire Final Environmental tmpact Report, including any
amendments necessitated by changes to the land use designations and goals,
policies and implementation measures. All members will then vote on the

Under this protocol, each decision affecting an abstention area will be considered
before the decision to adopt the general plan update, with each councilmember leaving
the room before any discussion or decision affecting an abstention area in which he or
she had a potential conflict of interest. Only after all decisions affecting the abstention
areas are made will the full City Council consider adoption of the general plan update in
its final form.
sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict of interest provisions are designed to ensure that public
officials will perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their
own financial interests or the financial interest of persons who have supported them.
(Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision when it is
"reasonably foreseeable" that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or
more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)
The Commission has adopted an eight-step analyical framework to determine whether a
public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a particular governmental
decision, which we apply below. (See Regulation 18700(bXl)-(8).)
Step One: Are Members of the City Council Public Officiats?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state
or local government agency." (Section 82048, Regulation 18701.) Members of the City
Council are therefore public officials within the meaning of the Act.2
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section S72OO ("872N filers" include city council
members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of eionomic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in Regulation
I 8702'5(bX I )(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for rhe
duration ofthe discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
Step Two: will the Members of the City Council be Making, participating in
Making, or Using their Positions to Influence a Governmental Decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation lS7OZ.l.)
A, public official "participates in making" a governmental decision when he or
she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations
regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2)3 A public official is using his or her official
position to "influence a govemmental decision" if he or she contacts or appears before, or
otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee, o, "orsuitant of the City
regarding the decisions. (Regulation 18702.3.)
The members of the City Council will be called upon to consider whether the City
should approve or disapprove amendments updating the City's general plan. Therefore
they will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using their oificial positions to
influence a governmental decision.
Step Three: Identifying the Councilmembers' Economic Interests-possible
Sources of Conflicts of Interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable the decision will have material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her-immediate
family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, (Section 87103; Regulations
18703-18703.5.) as follows:
an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (Section g7103(d);
Regulation I 8703. I (b));
an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or
indirect interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation lg:i1z.S,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)
I Exception: making or participating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a
public official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its presciiied
governmental function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official's wholly
owned real property or business entity. (See Regulation lgil}2.4)
a
a
a
a
an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income
that aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3);
an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87 103(e); Regulation 18703.4);
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediaie
family. This is also knows as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87103, Regulation I 8703.5.)
"Indirect investment or interest" means any investment or interest owned by a
business entity in which the official owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially a l0-percent
interest or greater. (Section 87103(e).)
Your question is confined to the sufficiency of a process designed to permit one
or more councilmembers who may have a conflict on interest in some portions of the
general plan update, due to potential effects on their economic interesti in real property,
to participate in final discussion and voting on the general plan update, after traving
recused themselves from discussion and voting on prior decisioni that might affect their
economic interests. Because the question concems only economic interesis in real
property, and you have not provided any facts concerning other possible economic
interests, our analysis is limited to the real property interests you posit.
Step Four: Are the Councilmembers'Economic Interests Directly or Indirectly
Involved in the Decisions You Describe?
R'eal property in which a public official has an economic interest is considered
directly involved in a governmental decision under any of the following circumstances:
o The property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the property
that is the subject of the decision. (Regulation tg7o4.Z(a)(l).)
o The decision involves a zoning or rezoning, annexation or de-annexation,
sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city, county,
district, or other local govemmental subdivision of the real property in
which the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real
property. (Regulation I 87 0a.2@)(2).)
r The decision involves the issuance, denial, or revocation of a license,
permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of the real
property in which the official has an inrerest. (Regulation Lg70a.2@)(3).)
a
a
The decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of any taxes
or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in which the official has
an interest. (Regulation 187 0a.2@)@).)
The decision is to designate the survey area, to select the project area, to
adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to certify the
environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory
to the redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above
decisions, and real property in which the official has an interest or any part
of it is located within the boundaries or the proposed boundaries of the-
redevelopment area. (Regulation 187 0a.2@)(5).)
The decision involves construction of or improvements to streets, water,
sewer, stonn drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which
the official has an interest will receive new or improved services.
(Regul ation 187 0a.2@)(6).)
Real property not directly involved in a govemmental decision is indirectly
involved. (Regulation 1870a(a).) Because we do not have sufficient information from
which to conclude that the councilmembers' economic interests in real property would be
indirectly involved in decisions concerning the general plan update, we-wii begin our
analysis from an initial presumption that all of the councilmembers' real propeiy
interests would be directly involved in decisions on the general plan update. 
-
We must, however, consider this presumption of direct involvement iri light of the
decisionmaking protocol you describe. You indicate that the City Council's process will
be divided into two parts: a public hearing phase and a decisionmaking, or voting, phase.
Regulation 187 04.2(b)(3) provides as follows :
'
"(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a) above, real property in which a
public official has an interest is not directly involved in a govemmental
decision, but is instead indirectly involved if:
t...)
"(3) The decision solely concems the adoption or amendment of a general
plan and all of the following apply:
"(A) The decision only identifies planning objectives or is otherwise
exclusively one of policy. A decision will not quarify under this
subdivision if the decision is initiated by the public official, by a person
that is an economic interest of the publit official, or by a person
representing either the public official or an economic interest of the public
official.
a


File No. ,{-09-079
"(B) The decision requires a further decision or decisions by the public
official's agency prior to implementing the planning or policy objectives.
Examples of further decisions include, but are not limited to, permitting,
licensing, rezoning, or the approval of or change to a zoning variance, land
use ordinance, or specific plan or its equivalent.
"(c) The decision does not concem an identifiable parcel or parcels or
development project. A decision does not "concern an identifiable parcel
or parcels" solely because, in the proceeding before the agency in which
the decision is made, the parcel or parcels are merely included in an area
depicted on a map or diagram offered in connection with the decision,
provided that the map or diagram depicts all parcels located within the
agency's jurisdiction and economic interests of the official are not singled
out.
"(D) The decision does not concern the agency's prior, concurrent, or
subsequent approval of, or change to, a permit, license, zoning
designation, zoning variance, land use ordinance, or specific plan or its
equivalent."
Under this exception, it appears that the councilmembers' real property would be
indirectly involved in the final, voting phase of the process you describe. Thus, so long
as the final discussion and vote is restricted to broad topics above the level of specific,
individual parcels of real property, the exception articulated by this regulation provides
that real property interests that are directly involved in earlier, more specific decisions,
are only indirectly involved in the final approval of the general plan update.a
Step Five: Material Financial Effects.
Regulation 18705.2(a)(l) provides that the financial effect of a govemmental
decision on real property directly involved in a governmental decision is presumed to be
material. Pursuant to the regulation, this presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is
not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect
at all on the real property.
Under Regulation 18705.1(bXl), the financial effect of a govemmental decision
on real property indirectly involved in a govemmental decision is presumed not to be
material. This presumption can be rebutted by proof that there are specific circum-
stances regarding the decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real property in
which the public official has an economic interest, that make it reasonably foreseeable
that the decision willhave a material financial effect on the real property in which the
'1 If for any reason this exception cannot be applied to an economic interest otherwise directly
involved in the final part of the decision, the official would have to recuse himself or herself from
participation in the final decisionmaking, as provided in Regulation 18702.5, outlined in footnote two
above.
public official has an interest. You provide no information indicating any specific
circumstances regarding these decisions that may rebut a presumption of non-materiality.
For purposes of our analysis we will therefore assume that no such circumstances exist.
In summary, for the economic interests in real property you describe, there are
rebuttable presumptions that the economic effect of a governmental decision will be
material, or will not be material, depending on whether the economic interest is directly
or indirectly involved in the decision.
Step Six: Reasonable Foreseeability.
Whether a financial effect of a govemmental decision is reasonably foreseeable at
the time the decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. The effect of
a decision is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered "reasonably foreseeable;" a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when
providing advice. (In re Oglesby, supra,l FPPC Ops. 71.)
Your request for advice appears to raise no question on the foreseeability of the
presumed financial effects of the decisions at issue. Our analysis therefore proceeds
under the assumption that the presumptive effects of these decisions are in all cases
"reasonably foreseeable," and will not be rebutted
Your account of the facts does not suggest any potential application of two
"exceptions" to the Act's conflict of interest provisiols treated, when appropriate, in
Steps 7 and 8 of the Commission's standard analysis.s What remains to be ionsidered,
then, is whether councilmembers who may have a presumptive conflict of interest in
decisions directly affecting economic interests in real property may recuse themselves
from those decisions at an early stage of the process leading up to final decisions on
approval of the entire general plan update, and yet be permitted to participate in the final
stages of the decisionmaking process.
The decisionmaking process you describe was created with an eye to Regulation
18709, which provides for "segmentation" of certain decisions, as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the
following conditions apply:
5 Thgse "exceptions" relate only to decisions that involve the "public generally" or the "legally
required participation" of a decisionmaker who would otherwise have a conflict of interest in the decision
at issue.


File No. ,{-09-079
"(l) The decision in which the official has a financiar interest can
be broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably
interrelated to the decision in which the official has a disqualifying
financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
segmented from the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's participation in any way; and
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest
has been madg, the disqualified public official's participation does not
result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from
which the official was disqualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are ,.inextricably
interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively determine,
affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision.
"(c) Budget Decisions and General Plan Adoption or Amendment
Decisions Affecting an Entire Jurisdiction: Once all the separate decisions
related to a budget or general plan affecting the entire jurisdiction have
been finalized, the public official may participate in the final vote to adopt
or reject the agency's budget or to adopt, reject, or amend the general
plan."
Under the process you have outlined, the City will "segment" decisions affecting
specific "abstention, areas" (property within 500 feet of a given official's real property
economic interest) from the overall general plan decision. Each decision affecting an
abstention area will be considered before the decision to adopt the general plan ufdates,
with each member leaving the room and not participating in any diicussion or decision
affecting the designated abstention area for which he or she has a conflict of interest.
Only after all decisions affecting the abstention areas have been resolved will the full
council convene to consider adoption of the general plan update.
The process proposed by the City meets each of the criteria outlined in Regulation
18709, and we conclude that all councilmembers, notwithstanding conflicts of interest in
decisions "segmented" as you describe, may participate in discusiion and voting on the
general plan updates at the conclusion ofthis process.
Regulation 18709(a)(3) requires that the decision in which an official has a
financial interest be considered "first." As we said in the Murphy Advice Letter, supra,
we interpret the word "first" in this regulation to mean simply that segmented decisions


Page No. l0
must be fully resolved before any final decision to "adopt, reject, or amend the general
plan'" More than one decision can be "segmented," so l'ong as they are decided in an
orderthat would prevent one decision from determining or'altering the result of one of
the other segmented decisions, each of which must of 
""ou.r. comply with the conditions
prescribed in Regulation 18709.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May members of the Riverbank City Council, who may have a conflict of interest
in some decisions related to changes in the land use element oithe proposed general plan,
participate in final decisions on approval of the general plan update and certif]cation of its
environmental impact report?

CONCLUSION: Yes. So long as the decisionmaking protocol you describe follows the dictates of
Regulation 18709 for "segmenting" decisions in which the members may have a conflict
of interest' they may participate in final decisions on approval of the general plan update
and certification of the associated environmental impaci report.
embedding.first_500_words: Re: Fnrn PolrrrcAl- PRACTTcEs CouurssroN .128 J Street o Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4_232g (916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886 April 16,2009 Douglas L. White Greenberg Traurig, LLP 2450 Colorado Ave, Suite 400 East Santa Monica, CA904O4 Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-09-079 Dear Mr. White: This Ietter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Riverbank regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ..Act"j-i ---- QUESTTON May members of the Riverbank City Council, who may have a conflict of interest in some decisions related to changes in the land use element oithe proposed general plan, participate in final decisions on approval of the general plan update and certif]cation of its environmental impact report? CONCLUSION Yes. So long as the decisionmaking protocol you describe follows the dictates of Regulation 18709 for "segmenting" decisions in which the members may have a conflict of interest' they may participate in final decisions on approval of the general plan update and certification of the associated environmental impaci report. FACTS The City of Riverbank is currently in the process of updating its General plan. city staff has prepared, and the City's planning Commissio, hr, ,""o*mended for approval, the certification of the proposed environmental impact report for the general ' Go'ernment Code sections 8 I 000-9 I 0 14. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109- I 8997, of the California Code of Regularions. {ts File No. 4-09-079 Page No. 2 plan update, as well as adoption of the general plan update itself. These two related decisions are hereafter described collectively as "the general plan update." The general plan update includes changes of the City's Land Use element. These changes include, among other things, modification of some zoning designations and the inclusion of minimum building densities. You believe that these ihanges have the potential to create a conflict of interest for one or more members of the City Council. The City has therefore developed a protocol for segmentation of the decisionmaking process under Regulation 18709, similar to the protocol discussed in the Murphy Advice Letter, No. 4-07-031. The goal is to allow any councilmember(s) who may hure u conflict of interest in certain specific decisions to participate in discussions and votes on final adoption of the general plan update. You describe the city's proposed decisionmaking protocol as follows: (l) Identification of Abstention Areas. The City would create and implement a procedure to separately manage City Council decisions affecting specific "abstention areas," defined as properties within 500 feet of real property in which a given councilmember has an economic interest. (2) Staff Presentation. When the general plan update agenda item is called, the public hearing will be opened. Staff (including the City's consultants) will give a presentation, at which time councilmembers may ask questions, but will not be expected or encouraged to express any opinions. (3) Public Testimony. After the staff presentation, public testimony will be heard, presenting evidence, argument and opinion on the
